pages.raw_text:
Tomorrow morning at the cham.
Manager j. G. Woodwork of the
Northern Pacific and Assistant
Traffic Manager w. P. Kenney of
the Great Northern will attempt
to explain to the shippers of Spo-
kane wherein the freight rates pro-
posed by their respective com.
panies. are better than those fixed
by the interstate commerce COM
mission.


Mr.. Woodwork told The Press
today that he had nothing to offer
for publication, but that they had
come on here to answer. any all
questions about the proposed rates,
which any merchant in Spokane
might ask. When told that there
seemed to be niger in the wood
pile, he retorted:


I'VE never made a freight rate
that would please everybody, and
do not expect to be able to do it
now"


Mr.. H. D. Turnkey, one of the
newly appointed members of the
committee, seemed to take the pro-
posed rates as joke, inasmuch as
the apparent concession of the rail
roads in reducing the tariff on
canned goods Ave cents per 100
west of St. Paul is more than Offset
by the proposed increase of tariff
on the same commodities east of
st. Paul.


A. w. Doland, in speaking for
the shippers, said that he had ne
idea what the railroads had to Of
fer new, but that it looked as #
they expected Spokane shippers to
believe a dollar bill as good as
a two dollar bill. There will De
a meeting Of the committee this
afternoon" he added, Ito seect
members to listen to the railroad
and if they are not
disposed to give us what we think
we ought to have, we have every
confidence we will get it in Wash
ington, where we will be repre-
scented by H. Nj. Stephens.
